headline: Alabama Opioid Settlement Fund to receive $43.1 million in 2026 supplemental budget
summary: The Alabama Oversight Commission on Opioid Settlement Funds approved a $43.1 million supplemental appropriation request for the FY 2026 General Fund budget, with approximately 60% allocated to the Department of Mental Health for opioid prevention, treatment, and recovery grants. The commission chair expects the budget to be ready for legislative consideration the first week of March. The funding derives from a $21 billion opioid settlement with pharmaceutical companies, spread over 18 years, rather than regular state revenues. This represents a targeted budget increase for public health initiatives, reflecting a 30% decline in opioid overdose deaths from September 2024 to September 2025. The supplemental appropriation process demonstrates how the state is managing special settlement funds within its broader budget framework.

headline: WV House Finance Committee mulls change to Hope Scholarship school voucher due to $230M price tag
summary: West Virginia's Hope Scholarship voucher program has ballooned to a $230 million cost, creating major budget pressure for the upcoming fiscal year. House Finance Committee Chairman Vernon Criss rejected Governor Morrisey's funding proposal as fiscally unworkable alongside pay raises and Medicaid increases. The Committee proposes changes to cut $20 million by reducing voucher amounts and banning out-of-state use. The Senate Finance Committee recommends funding the program exclusively through surplus funds instead of general revenue. The escalating costs have lawmakers warning the program could put the state budget "in the hole."

headline: San Diego teachers cancel strike as deadline neared; district to address demands over special education
summary: San Diego teachers canceled a planned Feb. 26 strike after reaching a tentative agreement with the school district to increase special education staffing and support, though pay raises are contingent on restoration of $5.6 billion in state funding that Gov. Newsom proposes withholding and would shortchange San Diego by about $85 million. The planned strike, which would have been the first in 30 years, was averted following teacher rallies and months of organizing around demands for special education funding and services. The agreement addresses caseload limits, provides stipends for oversized classes, and adds LGBTQ protections. The article also references a recent four-day San Francisco teacher strike with picket lines and rallies that secured $183 million in compensation improvements. The union stated it will continue advocating for equitable state education funding while the school board and union members vote to ratify the agreement.

headline: California school systems face red ink despite boosts in education spending
summary: Governor Newsom's proposed $88.7 billion K-12 budget for 2026-27 would delay $5.6 billion in constitutionally required Proposition 98 payments to close state deficits, effectively borrowing from schools. While per-pupil spending has increased 61% since Newsom took office to $27,418, California remains only middle-tier nationally. School districts face severe budget crises from declining enrollment and union-negotiated salary increases, with San Francisco, Sacramento, and Los Angeles Unified collectively facing hundreds of millions in deficits. The fiscal strain reflects structural challenges: demographic shifts reducing student populations, political pressure from unions, and the state's chronic $20 billion budget deficits. Raising per-pupil spending to top-tier levels would cost at least $30 billion annually, a funding gap the state cannot currently fill.

headline: Florida’s DeSantis jabs back at Beshear while in KY to push balanced budget amendment
summary: Kentucky's constitutional requirement to balance its budget was highlighted by Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis as a model of fiscal responsibility during his testimony supporting a national balanced budget amendment resolution, contrasting state fiscal management with federal spending practices. DeSantis praised Kentucky and Florida for "doing good things, certainly fiscally," while warning that a federal debt crisis would negatively affect state residents. The resolution, which received no committee action Wednesday, seeks to pressure Congress to adopt balanced-budget rules or trigger a constitutional convention. Democratic Rep. Anne Gay Donworth raised concerns that a convention could address issues beyond budgeting, while economists warn mandatory balanced budgets could worsen recessions by forcing spending cuts during downturns.

headline: KY House passes education bills. School officials support one. A union opposes the other.
summary: The Kentucky House passed House Bill 257 (92-1) to implement a new K-12 accountability and assessment system focused on measuring individual student growth rather than comparing cohort test scores. Supported by the Kentucky Department of Education, the bill incorporates community feedback and allows districts to spread testing throughout the year, earning bipartisan support from both a Republican assistant principal sponsor and a Democratic teacher representative.

headline: Hundreds pack Wilson County meeting to speak out against ICE detention facility
summary: Hundreds of Wilson County, Tennessee residents packed a county commission meeting to oppose a potential ICE detention center, with over 40 speakers addressing officials and overflow crowds filling hallways on two floors. The significant public gathering reflected community distrust of ICE transparency and concerns about infrastructure strain and reputational harm. Residents urged commissioners to enact blocking resolutions despite the county's lack of direct authority, while a sole supporter was met with chants of "shame, shame," demonstrating substantial civil engagement and political unrest.

headline: Immigration and Customs Enforcement reverses course, says it hasn’t purchased facility in Lebanon
summary: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) confirmed then retracted plans to purchase a detention facility in Lebanon, Tennessee, stating the initial confirmation was unauthorized. The facility was reportedly part of a $38.3 billion national plan to convert warehouses into large-scale immigrant detention centers. Wilson County officials, including the mayor and state representatives, said they had no prior knowledge of the plan. This follows a pattern of ICE proposing facilities without local notification in other states, including Oklahoma where community opposition led to project cancellation.

headline: Public gives resounding ‘no’ to proposed xAI Southaven permit
summary: A Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality public hearing on xAI's turbine permit demonstrated unanimous opposition from approximately 30 speakers, representing qualitative public input on environmental policy that could inform future opinion polling, though the article presents no formal survey data on broader state sentiment regarding the trade-off between economic development and air quality regulation.

headline: Republicans are pushing DHS over ICE warehouse purchases
summary: Sen. Roger Wicker successfully lobbied the Trump administration to abandon plans for an 8,500-bed immigrant detention center in Byhalia, Mississippi, after relaying opposition from local elected and zoning officials and raising economic development concerns. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem agreed to look elsewhere following Wicker's letter and phone call. A DHS spokesperson subsequently stated the agency had no detention centers to announce in Mississippi. The successful opposition mirrors similar Republican lawmaker concerns in other states about DHS converting warehouses into detention facilities without local consultation.

headline: Fiscal analysts project Michigan’s general fund could fall $475M short by 2028
summary: Gov. Whitmer's FY 2027-2028 budget proposal shows a $475 million general fund deficit but a $532 million school aid fund surplus, prompting a recommendation to withdraw $400 million from the Budget Stabilization Fund over House Speaker opposition. Federal One Big Beautiful Bill Act policies will cost Michigan an estimated $340 million in FY 28 while saving $70 million through reduced Medicaid and food assistance caseloads. The $88.1 billion proposal allocates $13.6 billion to the general fund and $21.4 billion to K-12 education, including a 2.5% per pupil increase. A major policy shift consolidates $1.4 billion for at-risk and English language learners into a weighted funding system, increasing district spending flexibility. The House Fiscal Agency informed the Appropriations Committee that legislative approval is required to access rainy day funds when state personal income declines.

headline: Lawmakers and faith leaders say ICE administration has no place in Southfield
summary: Elected officials, faith leaders, and Southfield residents protested ICE's new administrative office, fearing expanded enforcement in their diverse community and citing aggressive actions in other states; State Sen. Jeremy Moss highlighted three Senate bills to restrict ICE operations that are unlikely to advance in the Republican-controlled House, while U.S. Rep. Rashida Tlaib called to abolish ICE and impeach Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem.

headline: Fort Worth ISD students see gains. Will Texas keep the superintendent behind them?
summary: Fort Worth ISD Superintendent Karen Molinar reviewed a classroom ice breaker assignment at Mary Louise Phillips Elementary during the district's first day of school on Aug. 12, 2025, demonstrating direct administrative oversight of K-12 school operations.

headline: No vaccines, no safety net: NH’s child care centers are not ready for a measles outbreak
summary: New Hampshire's public health success in achieving zero measles cases in 2025 through 95% child care vaccination rates is threatened by multiple legislative bills. House Bills 1811, 1616, 1449, and 1719 would repeal immunization requirements for schools and child care centers. This risks disease outbreaks among vulnerable infants too young for full vaccination. It jeopardizes $40 million in federal child care funding. The legislation would reverse two decades of effective disease prevention policy and expose the state to severe economic costs.

headline: Bill wouldn’t invite federal immigration agents to SC. Opponents still worry.
summary: A South Carolina bill would mandate all law enforcement agencies operating jails sign 287(g) agreements with ICE, including a task force model allowing immigration status questioning during any police stop. Sponsored by Rep. Travis Moore, the legislation aims to standardize enforcement for undocumented immigrants in custody but faces intense opposition over concerns about eroding community trust, increasing local costs, and deterring crime reporting. While 37 agencies already have voluntary agreements, only Horry and York counties actively used them in 2025. Opponents including the ACLU warn the policy could strain police resources despite federal reimbursement bonuses tied to the number of undocumented immigrants identified. Following unanimous public testimony against the measure, Moore indicated he would reexamine potential costs and whether all three program models should be required.

headline: Key differences emerge in Missouri House, Senate bills to create A-F school grades
summary: Missouri House and Senate committees are advancing competing bills to implement an A-F grading system for public schools based on standardized test scores, responding to Gov. Mike Kehoe's executive order. The House version awards "A" grades to the top 10% of districts, removed provisions for automatically escalating benchmarks and penalties for low test participation, and delegates incentive funding criteria to education officials. The Senate version maintains a traditional percentage-based scale with escalating thresholds when 65% of schools achieve A/B grades and specifies $100 per student for top 5% performers. Democratic lawmakers and teachers' associations oppose the system, arguing it will disproportionately benefit wealthy districts and over-rely on high-stakes testing without local input. The legislation represents a major state-level push to reshape school accountability through standardized metrics and performance-based funding mechanisms.

headline: Will Bailey: Our Fragile State Is Too Dependent On The Kindness Of Billionaires
summary: The shift toward large-scale ownership in Hawaiʻi implies future state budget impacts through increased stewardship costs, potential land acquisition expenses, and policy trade-offs between conservation spending and other fiscal obligations.

headline: Moore signs bills banning agreements between local police, federal immigration agencies
summary: Governor Wes Moore signed emergency legislation banning 287(g) agreements between Maryland local police and ICE, requiring nine counties to terminate their agreements within 90 days. The law, effective immediately, faces potential legal challenges from sheriffs who argue it will increase immigration chaos by forcing ICE to conduct more public enforcement operations. Supporters, including Democratic leadership, contend the agreements enabled federal overreach and detained individuals without criminal histories, undermining constitutional policing. The bill was prioritized amid national backlash against aggressive immigration enforcement tactics, with Moore emphasizing that state and local resources should focus on public safety threats rather than civil immigration status enforcement. The policy shift follows years of advocacy by immigrant rights groups and represents a significant state-level response to federal immigration policy.

headline: Tension rising over how to spend revenue from proposed WA income tax
summary: Washington's proposed 9.9% millionaire income tax is projected to generate approximately $3.5 billion annually, with a political dispute over allocation between tax relief and budget stability; Governor Ferguson advocates for $1.9 billion in annual tax relief for lower-income families and small businesses, while Senate Majority Leader Pedersen warns that allocating more than 25-40% (about $1.4 billion) to tax cuts would eliminate budget "breathing room" and risk re-creating the multibillion-dollar shortfall that required emergency cuts and tax hikes last year, leaving the House to resolve the balance between affordability measures and fiscal sustainability.

headline: Tie vote stalls permanent sales tax reduction effort as proposed sales tax increase advances
summary: South Dakota's legislative budget committee adopted better-than-anticipated revenue projections, but lawmakers remain divided on tax policy's budget impact: a Senate proposal to permanently maintain the 4.2% sales tax rate stalled on a tie vote, while a House plan to raise the rate to 5% over two years would generate $337 million for property tax relief and potential funding increases for education, state employees, and Medicaid providers, though critics highlight existing budget constraints including lagging maintenance funding and below-inflation increases for key services.

headline: Senate and House budgets diverge as upper chamber rejects funding cuts
summary: The Wyoming Senate rejected the Joint Appropriations Committee's austerity budget by restoring over $253 million to align with Governor Gordon's recommendations, reversing unprecedented cuts to the University of Wyoming, Department of Health, economic development agency, and public media. Senate Majority Leader Tara Nethercott framed the restoration as reflecting "the will of the people," while the House Freedom Caucus defended its constitutional authority to control purse strings after defeating a similar amendment. The Senate completed its budget review efficiently with only 8 adopted amendments, whereas the House faced 122 amendments and lengthy debate, approving only minor funding additions. House critics argued the JAC process lacked transparency and adequate public testimony, while supporters maintained it represented weeks of deliberate committee work. This fundamental divergence between chambers creates a politically charged reconciliation process for the state's two-year budget.

headline: Four more measles cases confirmed in Penobscot County
summary: Maine CDC confirmed five measles cases in Penobscot County among unvaccinated individuals, with one hospitalization; while not meeting the state's outbreak definition, the agency established a new public dashboard to track cases, highlighting public health concerns amid over 900 cases across 24 states nationally in 2026 and marking Maine's first measles cases since 2019.

headline: Iowa bottle bill advocates say Iowans support expanded redemption process
summary: A Cleaner Iowa statewide survey found 72% of Iowans support the bottle bill and 64% report increased difficulty redeeming containers since 2022 legislation allowed retailer opt-outs, leaving 26 counties without redemption centers; the poll results align with House Study Bill 661 advancing from subcommittee to expand beverage container eligibility, though the bill faces potential cutoff via the legislative funnel.

headline: Board of Regents to consider 3% tuition hikes for some state university students
summary: Tuition increases proposed by Iowa's public universities would generate $38 million in new revenue, potentially offsetting state budget allocations for higher education amid uncertainty over state funding, with a new law mandating earlier approval to improve fiscal predictability.

headline: ICE detains four in Eau Claire County
summary: ICE agents detained four individuals in Eau Claire County with assistance from the sheriff's office and Altoona police; one was apprehended at a construction site while three others fled, trespassed into a Washington town residence, and barricaded themselves in a garage. After verbal requests failed, deputies used pepperballs to secure their surrender. All four individuals were turned over to ICE, and none were held at the Eau Claire County Jail. The incident demonstrates direct cooperation between local Wisconsin law enforcement and federal immigration authorities in apprehending and transferring individuals to ICE custody.

headline: GOP leaders propose tax relief compromise that leaves out money for general school aid
summary: Republican legislative leaders proposed a $2.3 billion package that includes $200 million for special education funding increases to 42% reimbursement in 2026 and 45% in 2027, but explicitly excludes general school aid increases, instead offering $500 million for property tax relief through the school levy tax credit. The proposal directly conflicts with Gov. Evers' request for $450 million in general school aid to offset his 400-year veto extending the $325 per pupil revenue limit increase, which Republicans argue creates a disincentive for school district efficiency and burdens taxpayers. Republican lawmakers stated that "no amount of funding increase can address the root causes of the education funding problem" and advocated for systemic reforms like school district consolidation rather than guaranteed funding. The plan also includes $1.4 million for income tax reductions on teacher expenses but prioritizes property tax credits over direct operational funding for school districts, drawing criticism from education advocates who call the approach "irresponsible and unacceptable." The proposal reflects a broader political impasse as the legislative session nears its end, with Republicans tying K-12 funding decisions to tax relief and program reforms while the governor pushes for direct aid to schools.

headline: Amid concern over cuts, Idaho Gov. Brad Little says setting the budget is the Legislature’s job
summary: The Idaho Legislature's budget committee approved permanent 5% across-the-board cuts for FY 2027 despite Governor Brad Little's opposition, who argues his own plan already balanced the budget. Little downplayed veto likelihood, citing political risks, election timing, and procedural constraints in the new bundled budget process that may prevent line-item vetoes. The cuts threaten wildfire response, mental health courts, tax refunds, and crisis systems. The governor's strategy appears focused on pressuring legislators, generating public awareness, and attributing responsibility to the legislature. With all 105 lawmakers and Little facing reelection, the budgets require full House and Senate approval before reaching his desk for signature, veto, or passive enactment.

headline: Burlington mayor signs executive order outlining local ICE protocols
summary: Burlington Mayor Emma Mulvaney-Stanak signed an executive order establishing citywide ICE response protocols that prohibit police from assisting civil immigration enforcement and require documentation of any federal actions. The order updates emergency management plans, alert systems, and community support resources while affirming constitutional protections for all residents. The City Council will introduce a supporting "ICE Out of Burlington" resolution opposing ICE operations, warrantless surveillance, and unauthorized entry into municipal property. The resolution has full board support and follows similar actions in Williston and South Burlington. These measures prepare for potential ICE escalation while maintaining public safety and community trust.

headline: Journalist Georgia Fort pleads not guilty to felony charges stemming from church protest
summary: Eight individuals, including journalists Georgia Fort and Don Lemon, pleaded not guilty to federal felony charges stemming from a Jan. 18 protest at a St. Paul church where demonstrators interrupted a service to demand justice for a woman killed by an ICE agent and protest a pastor's ICE affiliation during the Trump administration's "Operation Metro Surge." Prosecutors secured grand jury indictments after a judge refused to sign the charges, leading to Fort's arrest by DEA agents and condemnation from journalism organizations who called the prosecution an attack on press freedom and First Amendment rights.
